meta.title: Board Safety Report — FY2025 HSE Performance
meta.subtitle: For the Board of Directors · Annual · grounded in ISO 45001 9.1/9.3
meta.author: Head of HSE
meta.deid_notice: All incident detail in this report is de-identified AND aggregated — events are reported as counts and categories by role label only; no individual incident, injured party, exact date+location, or small (<5) cell is identifiable, and no re-identification key appears. Figures are decision-support; a competent person must review this report.

sections.text: FY2025 was a year of improving lagging performance against a rising exposure signal that the board must act on. Our LTIFR fell 18% to 4.92 (from 6.00 in FY2024) and now sits below the sector benchmark, but high-potential incidents rose 22% — the trend that matters most: we are getting fewer outcomes while the conditions that produce a fatality are becoming MORE frequent, concentrated in work-at-height and contractor-managed activities. The single decision this report asks the board to make is whether to fund the FY2026 work-at-height and contractor-assurance programme that the HiPo pattern says is now the highest-leverage control on serious-injury exposure.

The headline is not the falling injury rate — it is the rising HiPo/SIF precursor count. A lower LTIFR alongside more high-potential events is the classic signature of a serious event being set up. The board is asked to treat the work-at-height + contractor exposure as the year's governing risk.

Lagging rates (TRIR / DART / LTIFR) are computed deterministically by the shared incident_rates engine from the period's recordable counts and hours worked — they are not estimated in prose. Leading indicators are management-supplied. Each figure is read against the prior period and the benchmark in the Trend analysis below; the table here is supporting evidence, not the deliverable.

sections.caption: Period indicators vs prior period and benchmark. Benchmarks carry source + year; an unsourced comparator is flagged [GAP], never invented.

sections.text: The period's significant HSE events, rolled up to board altitude — counts and categories by role label, no individual incident narrated:

items: Recordable injuries: aggregated by mechanism — slips/trips/falls-same-level (largest category), manual-handling, and struck-by; no single category below 5 cases is published individually.
Lost-time injuries: down vs the prior period; all in operational and contractor roles (role labels only).
Notable near-misses: a cluster in work-at-height and mobile-plant interaction — the leading signal behind the HiPo rise.
Regulatory contacts: a small number of routine inspections, all closed; no enforcement notices.
Environmental events: none in scope this period (environmental metrics not supplied).

sections.text: Interpreting the high-potential and serious-injury-or-fatality precursor pattern (not merely listing it): the 22% rise in HiPo events is concentrated in two activities — unprotected or improvised work at height, and contractor-managed lifting/mobile-plant operations. These are textbook SIF precursors: the realistic worst-credible outcome of several of this year's near-misses was a fatality, even though the actual outcomes were minor. The reading for the board is that our exposure to a serious or fatal event has RISEN this year despite the falling injury rate, and that exposure is predictable — it lives in known activities and a known part of the workforce (contractors), which makes it controllable. This is the lens a lagging-rate table hides; it is the basis for the strategic decision below. (All HiPo/SIF inputs are de-identified and aggregated — no single event is narrated with date, location, or injury detail.)

LTIFR fell 18% vs FY2024 and now sits below the BLS SOII manufacturing sector average (5.4, 2023); TRIR fell 10% and is likewise below the 2.7 (BLS SOII, 2023) sector figure — defensible, sourced improvements. Leading indicators improved (training +6pp, audit closure +8pp) but audit closure (81%) remains below the 90% internal target — a gap the board should note. The counter-trend is the HiPo rise (+22%), which has no published benchmark and is tracked internally [GAP]; read together with the SIF-precursor concentration, the trends say outcomes improved while exposure to a serious event worsened.

rows.action: Approve and fund the FY2026 work-at-height assurance programme (engineered edge protection standard + competency verification) — the highest-leverage control on the SIF exposure the HiPo trend reveals.
Direct a contractor safety-assurance uplift (pre-qualification + active supervision standard) — contractor-managed activity is the second SIF-precursor concentration.
Set audit/inspection closure as a board-monitored objective until it reaches the 90% target (currently 81%).
Endorse continued HiPo/SIF-precursor reporting as a board-level leading indicator (it predicted this year's exposure shift the lagging rates missed).

sections.text: These are governance-level decisions (resource allocation, policy direction, accountability) tied to ISO 45001 9.3 management-review outputs — not operational CAPA task lists (those are owned by incident-investigation / capa-manager).

If the work-at-height and contractor programmes are funded in Q1, the HiPo/SIF exposure trajectory should bend back over FY2026 and the lagging gains consolidate. The emerging risks to watch are the audit-closure gap and any further HiPo concentration in new contractor scopes. The board's governing question for the next period remains exposure, not just outcomes: are we reducing the conditions that produce a serious event, not only the count of minor ones.
